figures.caption: Figure 1. A conceptual comparison of agent cognitive paradigms, contrasting existing approaches with our proposed value-driven autonomy. (Left) Task-driven agents are limited to passive instruction-following, acting only on external commands. (Center) Needs-driven agents are reactive, triggered by the immediate need to satisfy an internal state (e.g., a low “Cleanliness” metric). (Right) Our Valuedriven approach enables proactive autonomy. The agent possesses an internal framework of high-order values (e.g., “Stewardship”, “Security Environmental”). It resolves conflicting motivations by reasoning through abstract value trade-offs, allowing it to deliberate which action to prioritize (e.g., steadying a vase for “Security” vs. cleaning waste for “Stewardship”) to generate coherent, self-directed behavior.
Table 8. Action Space. The list of symbolic action primitives available to the planner, along with their functional descriptions and arguments.
Table 5. The Seven Intrinsic Value Dimensions. Definitions for the components of the agent intrinsic value system, used to form the persona vector w.
Table 6. Schwartz 10-to-7 Mapping. How the 10 basic Schwartz values are adapted into the 7 dimensions used in our household setting through retention, splitting, merging, and removal.
Figure 4. Analysis of State vs. Persona. (a) Our agent correctly modulates behavior based on internal status. (b) With identical status, different personas produce divergent, value-aligned behaviors.

Figure 5. Value Relationships and Trade-offs across Different Levels. The top row displays subgoal-level value gain relationships: (Top-left) A synergistic positive correlation between Security (Environmental) and Stewardship. (Top-right) A conflicting or independent relationship between Hedonism and Stewardship. The bottom row shows trajectory-level value trade-offs as persona weights are varied: (Bottom-left) A synergistic trajectory where Security (Environmental) and Stewardship value gains increase together. (Bottom-right) A conflicting trajectory demonstrating an explicit trade-off between Hedonism and Stewardship.

Figure 2. The ValuePlanner Architecture. Our hierarchical framework decouples abstract value reasoning from symbolic action grounding. The High-Level Value Reasoner deliberates on “what to do” to maximize value, while the Symbolic Action Grounder determines “how to do it” using classical planning.
